attributes.stem:
A three-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent concerned about a linear, raised, brown lesion on his right arm that has been present since birth. The lesion has grown proportionally with the child. His parents described a blue colouring to his buttocks at birth which had faded in the first year of life, thus they had expected this lesion to resolve as well. His mother has had a melanoma-in-situ excised, and his grandmother died of squamous cell carcinoma metastasis. He has eczema and uses regular steroid cream on the dry areas adjacent to the lesion and his flexures. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 5 cm linear, verrucous, hyperpigmented plaque following Blaschko's lines on the right upper arm. The lesion is not tender or pruritic. There are dry eczematous patches on his flexures and the remainder of the examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic epidermal naevus, which is a benign hamartomatous lesion characterised by its linear pattern following Blaschko's lines from birth or in the first year of life. It is a common presentation affecting approximately 1:1000 people and are often called 'birthmarks'. The lesion is asymptomatic and not causing any functional impairment. It is not related to eczema or his likely congenital dermal melanocytosis (mongolian spot).

Regular monitoring without intervention is the most appropriate management for an asymptomatic epidermal naevus that is not causing functional problems or significant cosmetic concern. These lesions are benign and typically grow proportionally with the child.

Immediate surgical excision would be indicated if the lesion was causing functional impairment, significant cosmetic distress, or if malignant change was suspected. In this case, there are no such indications for surgical intervention.

Topical corticosteroids are not effective for epidermal naevi as these are developmental anomalies rather than inflammatory conditions. Steroids may cause skin atrophy without addressing the underlying condition and it is not related to his eczema.

Laser therapy has limited efficacy in treating epidermal naevi and may result in scarring. It is sometimes considered for superficial lesions in cosmetically sensitive areas, but this is not indicated in this case.

Cryotherapy is not appropriate for epidermal naevi as it can cause scarring and hypopigmentation without effectively treating the condition. Whilst it is verrucous, it is not a viral wart. Cryotherapy is more commonly used for viral warts and other superficial skin lesions.

It is important to note that extensive epidermal naevi or those associated with other developmental abnormalities may be part of the epidermal naevus syndrome, which would require more comprehensive evaluation. However, in this case, the solitary lesion and absence of other abnormalities make this unlikely.
learningPoints: Epidermal naevi are benign hamartomas that are usually present at birth or appear in early childhood.
Surgical excision is the treatment of choice for localised epidermal naevi causing functional or cosmetic concerns.
Conservative management is appropriate for asymptomatic epidermal naevi without functional impairment.
references: Ryan E, Warren L. Birthmarks Identification and management. Australian Family Physician. 2012: 41 (5). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2012/may/birthmarks
Ngan V. Epidermal naevus. DermNet NZ. 2003.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/epidermal-naevi
